behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: The real failure here is that the AI model fails to identify all key terms, especially in complex contracts with unusual phrasing. A mock extractor will always return what you program it to return—it can't reveal whether the real model misses implicit obligations or unusual date constructions. This is fundamentally an AI capability problem, not an API-integration problem. Testing requires real AI calls on diverse test contracts to discover gaps in the model's extraction logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor on a corpus of real contracts (including edge cases: multi-party agreements, nested obligations, implicit dates). Verify that the output contains all key terms present in a manually-validated ground-truth annotation. Run multiple times if the model shows non-determinism. Failure mode: model consistently misses certain term types (e.g., penalty clauses buried in subsections).

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is that the AI doesn't understand indirect risk language—e.g., 'this arrangement may be subject to regulatory review' disguises a compliance gap. Mocking the risk assessor masks this entirely: you can program a mock to flag euphemisms, but the real model may miss them. This is an AI reasoning problem about language understanding, not a logic or API problem. Only real model calls on realistic risky language will surface when the model's risk classification breaks down.
behaviors.test_description: Create test contracts containing both direct risk language ('no refund guarantee') and indirect/euphemistic equivalents ('refund terms subject to [policy]'). Configure a real risk policy. Run the risk assessor on each contract. Verify that both direct and indirect problematic language are flagged. Failure mode: model flags direct risks but misses euphemistic variants, or flags unrelated neutral language.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on the AI's knowledge of regulatory requirements for different jurisdictions. You could test that the system loads jurisdiction configs and calls the compliance checker (Integration), but that won't catch if the model's understanding of UK GDPR, US state contracts, or Singapore financial requirements is incomplete or wrong. Real regulatory compliance is an AI knowledge problem, not an orchestration problem. Only real model calls on jurisdiction-specific test contracts reveal whether the model's regulatory knowledge is accurate.
behaviors.test_description: Assemble test contracts that should pass/fail regulatory checks for multiple jurisdictions (US, EU, UK). Provide correct regulatory requirements per jurisdiction. Run the compliance checker with each jurisdiction configured. Verify the model correctly determines pass/fail status. Failure mode: model misses a regulatory requirement for certain jurisdictions, or incorrectly assesses compliance due to incomplete legal knowledge.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: The core failure here is a merge logic bug: the code drops one assessment or loses the attribution. You can test this at Integration level by mocking the three reviewers to produce known conflicting outputs, then verifying the merge preserves both with correct source attribution. This is a deterministic logic problem—does the merge function correctly handle its inputs?—not an AI quality problem. However, real reviewer outputs might have unexpected formats/structures, so Agentic testing adds robustness.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock outputs from three reviewers for the same contract with designed conflicts (e.g., extractor marks clause as 'optional', risk assessor marks as 'high risk', compliance checker marks as 'compliant'). Pass both to the merge step. Verify the merged output contains both assessments with correct source attribution, not just the first or last one. Failure mode: merge silently drops one reviewer's assessment, or loses the source attribution.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires multiple concerns: (1) real parallelism (System-level: race conditions, timeouts on slow AI calls), (2) real AI outputs (Agentic-level: real model behavior on real contracts), and (3) orchestration correctness (Integration: merge logic). A pure Integration test with mocks won't catch concurrency bugs or how the system behaves under real model latency. A System or Agentic test alone misses either the parallelism aspects or the AI quality aspects. Only Workflow-level testing—real AI calls running in parallel on real contracts with full orchestration—can reveal whether the pipeline completes correctly without losing findings.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full pipeline on a real or realistic contract: dispatch to all three reviewers in parallel (real AI calls), collect results from all three, merge into a unified report. Run multiple times and verify: (1) all three reviewers are invoked, (2) no findings are lost during collection or merge, (3) the pipeline completes even if one reviewer is slow (timeout handling), (4) concurrent execution doesn't cause data races. Failure mode: race condition causes one reviewer's output to be overwritten, timeout on one reviewer drops its findings, or merge silently discards assessments under concurrent load.
